cuisine: Middle Eastern / South Asian (Singapore hawker adaptation)
region_of_origin: Middle East / South Asia; served in Singapore

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM covers meat-based mixed dishes; no dedicated kebab platter code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest defensible match applied.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard non-vegetarian kebab platter served in Singapore restaurants typically weighs approximately 300 g and includes 3–5 skewers of assorted grilled meats such as seekh kebab, shami kebab, and chicken tikka, accompanied by mint chutney and sliced onion.

nutrient_source.title: Non-Veg Kebab Platter — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010381 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated at 9.5 g/100g based on typical lamb/chicken kebab composition per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (USDA NDB #17174 ground lamb, cooked) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for a mixed non-vegetarian kebab platter. GI estimated at 30 based on the closest analogous food: grilled/roasted mixed meat dishes with minimal carbohydrate content. Grilled meat dishes with low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) have a GI in the range 25–35 per Atkinson et al. 2021 and Foster-Powell et al. 2002 (Am J Clin Nutr 76:5–56). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 5.24 × 3.0) / 100 = 4.7 (for a 300g serving). The glycemic load value is for the 300g serving size.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this dish in its standard preparation; if consumed, request significantly reduced salt across all kebab preparations and avoid sodium-containing chutneys, as a single 300 g serving delivers approximately 3240 mg sodium — well above the 2000 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) and low sugar (2.85 g/100g) are favourable, but high total fat (26.4 g/100g) including saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g) may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate and low glycaemic load (GL ~4.7 per 300 g serving) make this dish acceptable for Type 2 diabetes management in moderate portions, but the high saturated fat content should be monitored as it may worsen insulin resistance over time; avoid pairing with high-GI accompaniments such as naan, rice, or sweet chutneys.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4 g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g), estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g), cholesterol (85 mg/100g), and sodium (1080 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its high saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium content; if consumed, choose chicken-only kebab options to reduce saturated fat, request salt reduction, and limit portion size to no more than 150 g per occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (lamb/mutton) rich in arachidonic acid, high sodium (1080 mg/100g), estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g), and nightshade ingredients (green chili) collectively amplify inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid lamb and mutton kebabs due to their high arachidonic acid content which drives TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory activity; request chicken-only preparations, avoid green chili garnish, and note that the high sodium load worsens RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Non-Vegetarian Kebab Platter is a grilled mixed-meat dish served in Singapore's Middle Eastern and South Asian restaurants and hawker establishments. The platter typically comprises 3–5 skewers of assorted kebabs — commonly seekh kebab (minced spiced lamb or mutton on a skewer), shami kebab (pan-fried minced meat patties), and chicken tikka (marinated boneless chicken grilled on skewers) — accompanied by mint chutney, sliced raw onion, and lemon wedges. The meats are marinated in a blend of yogurt, ginger, garlic, cumin, coriander, garam masala, and green chili before being grilled over charcoal or in a tandoor oven, producing a smoky, charred exterior with a juicy, spiced interior. Nutritionally, the platter is high in fat (26.4 g/100g, predominantly from lamb/mutton fat and cooking oil), moderate in protein (10.7 g/100g), and low in carbohydrates (5.24 g/100g), making it a low-glycaemic-load dish. However, the sodium content is very high at 1080 mg/100g, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant salt reduction. The estimated saturated fat of ~9.5 g/100g and trans fat of ~0.2 g/100g are clinically relevant for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments and is gluten-free when served without bread accompaniments. It is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner and is popular across Singapore's multicultural dining landscape.
common_user_queries: Is kebab platter suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat kebab platter with high blood pressure?
Mixed meat kebab platter Singapore calories
Is kebab platter good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010381, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for ground lamb cooked, which is the closest approved-source analogue. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed journal. All other values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (ground lamb, cooked, NDB #17174) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. Trans fat (0.2 g/100g) is an estimate based on typical lamb/mutton fat composition; no direct measurement available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this mixed kebab platter are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture data not reported in HPB source for this item. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond those reported in the HPB record are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this specific dish as of 2026. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is plausible for a predominantly meat-based dish with minimal vegetable content. 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for a mixed non-vegetarian kebab platter exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Multilingual names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided as this dish is served in Singapore. The dish has Middle Eastern and South Asian culinary origins but is classified under Singapore's multicultural food landscape per HPB provenance. 6. Halal status is marked true as kebab platters in Singapore are predominantly served in halal-certified establishments; individual restaurant certification should be verified.
